content.content:
content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Accessibility FAQs | Isle of Man\
  \ Airport
- url: https://airport.im/contact-us/frequently-asked-questions/accessibility/
\
  - source: Isle of Man Airport
- domain: airport.im

## Content

# Accessibility
\
  
## Preparing to travel through Isle of Man Airport

Passengers looking for preparation\
  \ information are directed to the airport’s check-in guidance and its security\
  \ section.

## Requesting assistance

A passenger who wants to book assistance\
  \ for travel should contact the airline directly. The airport also links passengers\
  \ to its wider information for people travelling with reduced mobility or hidden\
  \ disabilities.

## Travelling with children

The airport notes that travelling\
  \ with children can be challenging and recommends preparing in advance to make the\
  \ airport experience less stressful.

## Disabled parking and terminal access
\
  
There is a disabled pick-up and drop-off zone at the front of the terminal building.
\
  
## Toilets

Toilet facilities are available on the first floor near the top\
  \ escalator and in the departure lounge.
"
metadata: {}
title: Accessibility FAQs | Isle of Man Airport
url: https://airport.im/contact-us/frequently-asked-questions/accessibility/

content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Accessibility for all Passengers\
  \ | Isle of Man Airport
- url: https://airport.im/your-journey/accessibility-for-all-passengers/
\
  - source: Isle of Man Airport
- domain: airport.im

## Content

# Accessibility\
  \ for all Passengers

## Assistance and accessibility

Isle of Man Airport states\
  \ that it is committed to providing an inclusive and welcoming environment for passengers\
  \ with visible and non-visible disabilities.

## Request assistance

Passengers\
  \ who require assistance when travelling through the airport should notify their\
  \ airline, tour operator or travel agent in advance. They should explain the assistance\
  \ they need and say whether they will travel with a mobility aid or an assistance\
  \ dog. Some airlines allow assistance to be added through the “My Booking”\
  \ area of their website.

The airline, tour operator or travel agent should be\
  \ notified no later than 48 hours before travel.

If a passenger arrives at the\
  \ airport without a prior assistance booking, the airport’s ground handling\
  \ agent, Menzies Aviation, will make every effort to accommodate the passenger’\
  s needs. The airport warns that passengers without a prior booking may wait longer\
  \ for assistance.

Before a departing flight, passengers should make themselves\
  \ known to the ground handling agent at their airline’s Check-In or Bag Drop\
  \ desk in the check-in hall. Ground staff will then finalise the appropriate assistance.
\
  
## Travelling with mobility equipment

Passengers planning to bring a wheelchair\
  \ or electric mobility aid should contact their airline at least 48 hours before\
  \ departure. Once the assistance and equipment information has been booked through\
  \ the airline or travel agent, it is passed to Menzies Aviation so that the handling\
  \ team can prepare.

A passenger may use their own wheelchair or electric mobility\
  \ aid as far as the aircraft, or may use an airport wheelchair.

For arriving\
  \ flights, Menzies Aviation will provide the passenger’s own wheelchair or\
  \ electric mobility aid, or an airport wheelchair, at the aircraft. The passenger\
  \ may transfer to their own equipment later in the arrival journey.

## Hidden\
  \ Disabilities Sunflower

The airport participates in the Hidden Disabilities\
  \ Sunflower scheme. A Sunflower lanyard is a discreet signal that a passenger may\
  \ need additional support or understanding. Free lanyards are available from the\
  \ Information Desk.

The airport describes the scheme as relevant to a range of\
  \ non-visible disabilities, illnesses and neurodiversity, including autism, dementia,\
  \ anxiety disorders, sensory processing disorders and ADHD.

A passenger wishing\
  \ to arrange a pre-flight familiarisation session should email Admin.Airport@gov.im.\
  \ Familiarisation is presented as an advance airport visit or preparation arrangement.
\
  
## Carrying medication

Liquid medication above 100 ml must be accompanied by\
  \ evidence that it has been prescribed to the passenger, such as a doctor’\
  s letter or a copy of the prescription. Security staff will check this evidence.
\
  
## Assistance through security

Passengers with visible or non-visible disabilities\
  \ who need help at security should make themselves known to a security officer.\
  \ The request can be handled discreetly. Security officers can help passengers prepare\
  \ for screening and can assist with lifting baggage onto the X-ray belt.

Passengers\
  \ are normally asked to remove coats, jackets and hats. A passenger who would be\
  \ uncomfortable with this can ask for a supervisor and request a search in a private\
  \ location.

Passengers should tell security staff about medical devices and where\
  \ they are located. Spare devices should be removed from baggage for screening.\
  \ Security officers can make reasonable adjustments after discussion with the passenger,\
  \ while still completing the screening required for entry to the security-restricted\
  \ area.
"

title: Accessibility for all Passengers | Isle of Man Airport
url: https://airport.im/your-journey/accessibility-for-all-passengers/

Your trip is:

- **Airline:** Loganair, flight **LM672**
- **Departure:** Isle of Man Airport (IOM) — **12 July 2026 at 09:10**
- **Arrival:** Manchester (MAN) — **09:55**
- **Booking:** Confirmed, with **one checked bag**

Your booking has **no advance mobility-assistance record**, so the applicable process is the airport’s **operational fallback**. Because the flight is tomorrow, the normal 48-hour advance-notification window has passed, but you should still contact **Loganair immediately** and explain the assistance you need, including any wheelchair or electric mobility aid.

### At Isle of Man Airport

1. **Allow extra time**—the airport warns that passengers without a prior assistance booking may have to wait longer.
2. Go to the **Loganair check-in or Bag Drop desk in the check-in hall** and clearly identify yourself as needing mobility assistance.
3. Airport ground-handling staff—**Menzies Aviation**—will then finalise and provide the appropriate assistance.
4. You may use your own wheelchair or mobility aid as far as the aircraft, or request an airport wheelchair.
5. If you need help at security, tell a security officer; the airport says assistance and reasonable screening adjustments can be arranged.

The airport also has an **Information Desk** offering general assistance and free **Hidden Disabilities Sunflower** lanyards if relevant. Its official guidance is available here: [Accessibility for all Passengers](https://airport.im/your-journey/accessibility-for-all-passengers/).

At Manchester, assistance arrangements are not recorded in the available trip information. When checking in at IOM, ask Loganair to confirm how your assistance will be handled on arrival at MAN.
